meta.routing_notes: The transcript features a back-and-forth exchange between Kamala Harris and Donald Trump, with a moderator facilitating the discussion. The content includes direct questions, rebuttals, and responses typical of a debate format.

A.reason: The speaker is advocating for a position on race and abortion, defending their stance against an opponent, and proposing policy related to Roe v. Wade, which aligns with the role of a candidate in a debate.

B.reason: The speaker is advocating a position on abortion, discussing their views on a political opponent, and expressing opinions on state rights and legislation, which aligns with the role of a candidate or debater.

C.reason: The speaker welcomes the participants and asks questions, which indicates a moderating role.

D.reason: The speaker cites specific statements made by President Trump and references the context of the election, indicating they are analyzing and reporting on the situation rather than participating as a candidate or moderator.

definitions.fakeness_score: Probability-like truth-risk that a statement is false or misleading, based on verification of factual accuracy and claim reliability using web sources. 0–1. Null if verification did not run or no checkable claim exists.
definitions.bias_score: Degree of linguistic/ideological bias in phrasing, framing, exaggeration, loaded language, or manipulative rhetoric. 0–1. This is NOT about factual truth.
definitions.political_leaning: Classification of overall political orientation in the segment (Left, Right, Neutral, Not Applicable).
definitions.emotion_tone: Dominant emotional tone expressed (categorical label, e.g., calm, angry, fearful, sarcastic, critical).
definitions.overall_misleading_index: Composite measure summarizing misleadingness. If fakeness_score exists: average(bias_score, fakeness_score). Else: equals bias_score.
definitions.model_agreement_score: Agreement between two independent model scoring passes (0–1). Reported when a second analysis model is enabled. Computed from similarity of bias scores and categorical matches (leaning/kind).
definitions.analysis_confidence: Confidence (0–1) in NON-verification outputs: bias_score, emotion_tone, segment_kind, stance fields, political_leaning.
definitions.fakeness_confidence: Confidence (0–1) in verification result ONLY. Present only when fakeness_score exists.
definitions.verification_verdict: One of: supported, contradicted, mixed, insufficient. Only present if verification ran.
definitions.segment_kind: One of: factual_claim, policy_claim, values_statement, question, personal_attack, analysis, descriptive, other.
definitions.stance_target: Entity/issue being discussed (string) or null if unclear.
definitions.stance_direction: One of: pro, anti, neutral/unclear (relative to stance_target).
definitions.sources: Verification sources used for fakeness_score (hard-capped by MAX_SOURCES_PER_VERIFIED_SEGMENT).
definitions.speaker_roles: Per-speaker inferred role: candidate/debater, moderator/host, journalist/analyst, commentator, audience, narrator, other.
definitions.speaker_selection: Speakers to score are selected automatically. Moderators/hosts/journalists/audience/narrator are excluded from scoring. Debate content requires at least 2 substantive debaters; fallback selects top talk-time speakers if needed.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: There is no state in this country where it is legal to kill a baby after it's born.

scores.verification_reasoning: The claim is accurate. All states in the U.S. have laws that protect infants after birth, making it illegal to kill a baby post-birth. The Born-Alive Infants Protection Act of 2002 affirms that any infant born alive, regardless of the circumstances, is considered a person under U.S. law and is entitled to legal protections. Additionally, the Unborn Victims of Violence Act recognizes a 'child in utero' as a legal victim if injured or killed during the commission of certain federal crimes, further emphasizing the legal protections afforded to both unborn and born infants. Therefore, the assertion that no state allows the killing of a baby after birth is supported by federal and state laws.
scores.claim_text: There is no state in this country where it is legal to kill a baby after it's born.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: body next. That is immoral. And I pledge to you When Congress passes a bill to put back in place the protections of Roe v. Wade, as President of the United States, I will proudly sign it into law. But understand, if Donald Trump were to be re-elected, he will sign a national abortion ban.

scores.claim_text: I will proudly sign [a bill to put back in place the protections of Roe v. Wade] into law.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: It's a lie. I'm not signing a ban and there's no reason to sign a ban because we've gotten what everybody wanted. Democrats, Republicans and everybody else. And every legal scholar wanted it to be brought back into the states, and the states are voting, and it may take a little time, but for 52 years this issue has torn our country apart, and they've wanted it back in the states, and I did something that nobody thought was possible. The states are now voting. What she says is an absolute lie. And as far as the abortion ban, no, I'm not in favor of abortion ban, but it doesn't matter because this issue has now been taken over by the states.

scores.verification_reasoning: As of March 6, 2026, several U.S. states are in the process of voting on abortion-related measures. For instance, Nevada's Question 6, which seeks to enshrine the right to abortion up to fetal viability in the state constitution, is scheduled to appear on the ballot on November 3, 2026. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Nevada_Question_6?utm_source=openai)) Additionally, Nebraska's Initiative 434, aiming to prohibit abortions after the first trimester, was approved by voters on November 5, 2024. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_Nebraska_Initiative_434?utm_source=openai)) Therefore, the claim that states are currently voting on abortion-related measures is accurate.
scores.claim_text: The states are now voting on the issue of abortion.

segments.speaker: A

You will not hear him talk about your needs, your dreams and your desires. And I'll tell you, I believe you deserve a president who actually puts you first and I pledge to you that I will.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: We have the biggest rallies, the most incredible rallies in the history of politics. That's because people want to take their country back.

sources.title: Millions turn out for anti-Trump 'No Kings' rallies

scores.verification_reasoning: While the speaker's rallies may be large, they are not the largest in political history. For instance, the 'No Kings' protests on October 18, 2025, drew nearly 7 million participants across over 2,500 locations, making it one of the largest single-day protests in U.S. history. Additionally, the March for Israel in Washington, D.C., on November 14, 2023, attracted 290,000 attendees, marking it as the largest pro-Israel gathering in U.S. history. These examples indicate that the speaker's rallies are not the largest in political history.
scores.claim_text: We have the biggest rallies in the history of politics.

sources.title: FBI Releases 2023 Crime in the Nation Statistics
FBI Releases 2024 Reported Crimes in the Nation Statistics

scores.verification_reasoning: Recent FBI reports indicate a decline in both violent and property crime rates in the U.S. in 2023 and 2024. Specifically, violent crime decreased by 3% in 2023 and 4.5% in 2024, with significant reductions in murder, rape, robbery, and aggravated assault. Property crime also declined by 2% in 2023 and 8.1% in 2024. These trends suggest that the claim of a significant increase in crime rates is not supported by the available data.
scores.claim_text: Crime here is up and through the roof.

sources.title: FBI Releases 2023 Crime in the Nation Statistics

scores.verification_reasoning: The FBI's 2023 Crime in the Nation statistics indicate a 3% decrease in national violent crime compared to 2022. This aligns with the claim that the FBI reports a decrease in overall violent crime in the U.S. in 2023.
scores.claim_text: the FBI says overall violent crime is actually coming down in this country

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: Donald Trump was fired by 81 million people. So let's be clear about that. And clearly he is having a very difficult time processing that.

scores.verification_reasoning: The claim that Donald Trump was 'fired' by 81 million people is a figurative expression referring to the 81 million votes cast for Joe Biden in the 2020 U.S. presidential election. While the term 'fired' is not a formal term in the U.S. electoral process, it is sometimes used colloquially to describe the outcome of an election where an incumbent president is not re-elected. In this case, Joe Biden received 81 million votes, the most ever cast for a presidential candidate, and won the Electoral College with 306 votes to Trump's 232, leading to Trump's removal from office. Therefore, the claim is supported by the election results, though the term 'fired' is not an official term in the electoral process. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election?utm_source=openai))
scores.claim_text: Donald Trump was fired by 81 million people.
